description: Mapping of PDPA 2010 (Act 709) provisions to international data protection frameworks: EU GDPR, APEC CBPR, and ISO 27701
disclaimer: These mappings are indicative only. The PDPA and international frameworks have different scopes, definitions, and legal effect. The mappings are provided for comparative purposes to assist practitioners in multi-jurisdictional compliance programs.

mappings.notes: Both frameworks define core concepts including personal data, processing, data subject, data controller (data user in PDPA), and data processor. Key differences: PDPA uses 'data user' where GDPR uses 'controller'; PDPA 'data processor' aligns with GDPR 'processor'. PDPA's definition of personal data is substantially similar to GDPR's though somewhat narrower in its treatment of pseudonymous data.

Both require a lawful basis for processing. GDPR provides 6 bases; PDPA s6(2) has equivalent bases. Key differences: GDPR has a specific basis for 'public task' which is narrower in PDPA; GDPR's 'legitimate interests' balancing test is substantively equivalent to PDPA's post-2024 amendment version; PDPA originally lacked the explicit 'legitimate interests' basis which was added by A1699.

mappings.gdprName: Information to be provided to data subjects
mappings.notes: Both require proactive information provision to data subjects at collection. GDPR requires additional information including the legal basis, DPO contact details, and retention periods. PDPA's notice requirements are somewhat less extensive but the 2024 Amendment and DPN Guideline have narrowed the gap considerably.

The Disclosure Principle aligns with GDPR's purpose limitation principle. Both restrict processing and disclosure to specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes. GDPR provides an additional 'compatible purposes' framework for secondary uses, which PDPA does not explicitly address, though this gap is partially addressed in the General Code 2022.

Both require appropriate technical and organizational measures proportionate to the risk. GDPR explicitly requires consideration of state of the art, implementation costs, and nature of risks. PDPA's Security Standards 2015 provide more prescriptive baseline requirements, whereas GDPR adopts a more risk-based and technology-neutral approach.

Both prohibit retention beyond the period necessary for the processing purpose. The concepts are functionally equivalent. PDPA's Retention Standard 2015 provides more prescriptive timelines, which can be seen as both a strength (certainty) and a limitation (less flexibility for risk-based approaches).

Both require that personal data be accurate and kept up to date. GDPR explicitly adds 'erased or rectified without delay' for inaccurate data. PDPA's Data Integrity Standard 2015 provides substantive implementation requirements equivalent to GDPR's accuracy principle.

mappings.gdprName: Right of access by the data subject
mappings.notes: Both provide data subjects with a right to access their personal data. Key differences: GDPR includes a right to data portability (Article 20), which PDPA does not currently have. GDPR provides a right to erasure ('right to be forgotten', Article 17), which PDPA does not explicitly provide. The PDPA access right is procedurally similar but the scope of accompanying information required is less extensive than under GDPR.

mappings.gdprName: Processing of special categories of personal data
mappings.notes: Both identify special categories of data requiring enhanced protection. The categories are largely consistent: health data, racial/ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, trade union membership, and criminal convictions. Key differences: GDPR includes genetic data and biometric data as special categories; PDPA does not explicitly categorize these but may include them by interpretation. GDPR has 10 grounds for processing special categories; PDPA's grounds are narrower.

mappings.gdprName: Transfers of personal data to third countries or international organisations
mappings.notes: Both establish a framework for international data transfers based on adequacy, appropriate safeguards, and derogations. Key differences: GDPR's adequacy mechanism (Article 45) is managed by the European Commission; PDPA's adequacy list is determined by the Malaysian Minister on the Commissioner's recommendation. GDPR's Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) find equivalents in PDPA's post-2024 Amendment mechanisms. GDPR's BCR framework is more mature and the SCCs are more widely used internationally.

mappings.gdprName: Designation, position and tasks of the data protection officer
mappings.notes: Both establish mandatory DPO requirements for certain processing activities. The criteria for mandatory appointment are broadly comparable (large-scale processing of special categories, large-scale systematic monitoring). Key differences: GDPR's DPO appointment criteria are more clearly delineated; PDPA leaves more to the Commissioner's discretion via guidelines. GDPR's DPO must have expert knowledge of data protection law; PDPA's qualification criteria are similar but defined by the DPO Guideline 2025.

mappings.gdprName: Notification of a personal data breach to the supervisory authority; Communication of a personal data breach to the data subject
mappings.notes: Both require notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours and to data subjects for high-risk breaches. The threshold concepts are similar: GDPR uses 'likely to result in a risk to rights and freedoms'; PDPA uses 'notifiable data breach' defined in the Act and DBN Guideline. Key differences: PDPA's 7-day deadline for data subject notification is less prescriptive than GDPR's 'without undue delay'; GDPR's content requirements for notifications are more detailed.

Both establish an independent supervisory authority with investigatory and enforcement powers. Key differences: GDPR requires supervisory authority independence from government influence; PDPA's Commissioner is appointed by the Minister, which creates some concerns about formal independence. GDPR provides for the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) for cross-border cooperation; PDPA has no formal international supervisory cooperation mechanism.

The PDPA Notice and Choice Principle closely mirrors the APEC Notice principle. Both require clear notification to individuals about the purposes of data collection, types of data collected, and the organizations collecting the data. The bilingual notice requirement under PDPA is a Malaysia-specific implementation.
Both limit collection to data necessary for specified purposes with appropriate consent. The APEC principle aligns with PDPA's purpose limitation embedded in the General Principle.

mappings.pdpaName: General Principle and Notice and Choice Principle
mappings.notes: APEC requires that use be limited to specified purposes with appropriate consent for secondary use. PDPA's General and Disclosure Principles together implement equivalent restrictions.

The APEC Choice principle requires mechanisms for individuals to control the use and disclosure of their data. PDPA's Disclosure Principle and s43 (opt-out from direct marketing) together implement the Choice principle.
Direct equivalence. Both require data to be accurate, complete, and current for the purposes for which it is used.

Both require appropriate security safeguards proportionate to the harm that could result from unauthorized access or misuse. The PDPA Security Standards 2015 provide more prescriptive implementation requirements than APEC's principle-based framework.
Both provide individuals with rights to access and correct their personal data. Implementation mechanisms are similar.

APEC's Accountability principle requires organizations to have a designated individual responsible for compliance and to have mechanisms for redress. PDPA implements this through the Commissioner's enforcement powers, mandatory registration, and codes of practice, rather than through a strictly company-level accountability framework.

The CBPR system is specifically designed to enable cross-border data flows within APEC while maintaining privacy standards. PDPA's s129A cross-border transfer framework is consistent with CBPR principles. Malaysia's participation in CBPR or its successor, the Global Cross-Border Privacy Rules (GCBPR) framework, would facilitate recognition of CBPR-certified transfers.

iso27701.fullTitle: Security techniques — Extension to ISO/IEC 27001 and ISO/IEC 27002 for privacy information management — Requirements and guidelines

mappings.isoClause: 6.1 (Information security risk assessment with privacy considerations)
mappings.notes: ISO 27701 extends ISO 27001's risk management approach to privacy. Clause 6.1 requires organizations to identify and assess information security risks that include privacy risks. PDPA's Security Principle and the Security Standards 2015 align with this risk-based approach.

ISO 27701 Clause 6.4 introduces privacy-specific risk assessments. Organizations implementing PDPA's Security Principle through the Security Standards 2015 should conduct ISO 27701-aligned privacy impact assessments, particularly for new processing activities.

mappings.isoClause: 7.3 (Determine when and how to obtain consent)
mappings.notes: ISO 27701 Clause 7.3 addresses consent management as a component of privacy information management. The PDPA Notice and Choice Principle, implemented through the DPN Guideline, aligns with these requirements.

ISO 27701 Clause 7.4 implements the privacy by design concept. While PDPA does not have an explicit 'privacy by design' obligation, the combined effect of the seven principles, read with the Security Standards and Codes of Practice, establishes an equivalent framework.

mappings.isoClause: 7.3.6 (Providing mechanism to modify or withdraw consent); 7.3.7 (Complying with data subject requests)
mappings.notes: ISO 27701 provides specific controls for managing data subject requests and consent changes. PDPA's data subject rights provisions (access, correction, opt-out) and the associated procedural requirements in the General Code 2022 align with these ISO controls.

mappings.isoClause: 6.13.1 (Management of information security incidents, including privacy breaches)
mappings.notes: ISO 27701 extends ISO 27001's incident management controls to address privacy breaches. Organizations implementing ISO 27701's incident management controls will have the foundational capabilities required to comply with PDPA's mandatory data breach notification obligations.

ISO 27701 Clause 8.5 addresses privacy-specific controls for third-party data sharing and cross-border transfers. ISO 27701 certification can serve as evidence of 'adequate safeguards' for PDPA cross-border transfer purposes, supporting the 'appropriate safeguards' mechanism under s129A as amended by A1699.

ISO 27701 requires clear assignment of privacy-related roles and responsibilities. The DPO role required under PDPA s129B can be mapped to the 'Privacy Officer' or 'Privacy Information Manager' roles in ISO 27701's organizational responsibility framework.

ISO 27701 certification provides a structured management system approach to PDPA compliance. Organizations certified under ISO 27701 will have addressed most of the technical and organizational requirements under the PDPA principles. The Commissioner recognizes ISO 27701 as a relevant international standard for PDPA compliance purposes.
